tips.action: Stand up and do 10 shoulder rolls, 5 each direction.
tips.science: Prolonged sitting increases cardiovascular risk by 14% per hour beyond 8 hours (Katzmarzyk et al., 2009).

tips.action: Drink a full glass of water right now.
tips.science: Even 1-2% dehydration impairs cognitive performance and mood (Ganio et al., 2011).

tips.action: Do 20 seconds of wrist circles — both directions.
tips.science: Microbreaks of 20-30 seconds reduce musculoskeletal discomfort in computer users (McLean et al., 2001).

tips.action: Walk to the farthest restroom or water station in your building.
tips.science: Incidental physical activity throughout the day is as beneficial as structured exercise for cardiometabolic health (Stamatakis et al., 2019).

tips.action: Check your posture: ears over shoulders, shoulders over hips.
tips.science: Forward head posture increases cervical spine load by 4.5 kg per inch of protrusion (Hansraj, 2014).

tips.action: Set a 'no caffeine after 2 PM' rule for today.
tips.science: Caffeine has a 6-hour half-life; afternoon consumption reduces deep sleep by 20% (Drake et al., 2013).

tips.action: Do 5 deep belly breaths: inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 6.
tips.science: Extended exhale activates parasympathetic nervous system, reducing heart rate within 30 seconds (Gerritsen & Band, 2018).

tips.action: Stretch your hip flexors — 30 seconds each side, lunge position.
tips.science: Seated hip flexion shortens the psoas, contributing to lower back pain in desk workers (Kendall et al., 2005).

tips.action: Name one emotion you're feeling right now. Just name it.
tips.science: Affect labeling reduces amygdala reactivity by up to 43% (Lieberman et al., 2007).

tips.action: Write down one thing that went well today, no matter how small.
tips.science: The 'Three Good Things' exercise increases happiness and decreases depression for 6 months (Seligman et al., 2005).

tips.action: If you're stuck on a problem, say out loud: 'This is hard, and that's okay.'
tips.science: Self-compassion during difficulty reduces cortisol and increases problem-solving persistence (Neff et al., 2007).

tips.action: Close your eyes for 30 seconds and notice 3 sounds around you.
tips.science: Mindful attention to present-moment sensory input reduces rumination (Jain et al., 2007).

tips.action: Rate your stress 1-10. If above 7, take a 5-minute walk before continuing.
tips.science: Self-monitoring of stress levels improves emotional regulation and early intervention (Tugade & Fredrickson, 2004).

tips.action: Text or message someone you trust: 'Hey, how's your day going?'
tips.science: Social support seeking reduces perceived stress even before the support is received (Cohen & Wills, 1985).

tips.action: What's the ONE thing that matters most today? Write it on a sticky note.
tips.science: Implementation intentions ('if-then' planning) increase goal attainment by 2-3x (Gollwitzer, 1999).

tips.action: Set a hard stop time for today. Put it in your calendar now.
tips.science: Psychological detachment from work during off-hours predicts lower exhaustion and higher well-being (Sonnentag & Fritz, 2007).

tips.action: Identify one task you can delegate or drop entirely this week.
tips.science: Job demands without corresponding resources lead to burnout via the health impairment pathway (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007).

tips.action: Ask yourself: Am I doing this because it matters, or because it's urgent?
tips.science: People systematically over-prioritize urgent tasks over important ones — the 'mere urgency effect' (Zhu et al., 2018).

tips.action: Block 25 minutes for focused work with no Slack/email. Start now.
tips.science: Task switching costs 23 minutes of refocus time per interruption (Mark et al., 2008).

tips.action: Say 'no' to one meeting this week that doesn't require your presence.
tips.science: Meeting overload is a primary driver of time poverty and reduced autonomy (Rogelberg et al., 2007).

tips.action: Send a 2-sentence appreciation message to a colleague today.
tips.science: Expressing gratitude strengthens social bonds and increases both giver and receiver well-being (Algoe et al., 2008).

tips.action: Eat lunch with someone (in person or virtual) instead of at your desk.
tips.science: Social eating increases feelings of belonging and cooperation (Dunbar, 2017).

tips.action: Reach out to one person you haven't spoken to in 2+ weeks.
tips.science: Weak ties (infrequent contacts) provide novel information and perspective more than strong ties (Granovetter, 1973).

tips.action: In your next meeting, ask someone a genuine question about their weekend.
tips.science: Self-disclosure reciprocity builds interpersonal trust and closeness (Aron et al., 1997).

tips.action: If you're working remotely, turn your camera on for the next call.
tips.science: Visual cues (facial expressions, gestures) account for 55% of communication and reduce misunderstanding (Mehrabian, 1971).

tips.action: Spend 5 minutes reading something completely unrelated to your current task.
tips.science: Cross-domain exposure increases creative problem-solving through analogical transfer (Gentner et al., 2003).

tips.action: Write down one thing you learned today, even if it's trivial.
tips.science: Retrieval practice (writing from memory) strengthens long-term retention by 50%+ vs. re-reading (Roediger & Butler, 2011).

tips.action: Teach a concept you learned this week to someone else (or explain it out loud).
tips.science: The protege effect: teaching material deepens understanding more than studying alone (Chase et al., 2009).

tips.action: Listen to a podcast or TED talk during your next commute or walk.
tips.science: Audio learning during routine activities converts 'dead time' into growth time without additional cognitive load (Mayer, 2009).

tips.action: Try solving today's problem with a tool or method you've never used before.
tips.science: Deliberate practice in unfamiliar domains builds cognitive flexibility and adaptive expertise (Ericsson et al., 1993).

tips.action: Look out a window for 20 seconds. Focus on the farthest point you can see.
tips.science: Distance gazing relaxes the ciliary muscle and reduces digital eye strain (Sheppard & Wolffsohn, 2018).

tips.action: Open a window or step outside for 60 seconds of fresh air.
tips.science: Indoor CO2 above 1000 ppm reduces cognitive function by 15-50% (Allen et al., 2016).

tips.action: Clear one unnecessary item from your desk right now.
tips.science: Visual clutter competes for attention and reduces working memory capacity (McMains & Kastner, 2011).

tips.action: Adjust your screen brightness to match the ambient light in your room.
tips.science: Screen-to-environment contrast above 3:1 increases eye fatigue and headache frequency (Osterhaus et al., 2015).

tips.action: If you don't have a plant near your workspace, consider getting one this week.
tips.science: Indoor plants reduce stress biomarkers (cortisol) by 12% and increase productivity by 15% (Nieuwenhuis et al., 2014).

tips.action: Check: Is there one subscription you're paying for but haven't used in 30 days?
tips.science: The sunk cost fallacy keeps people paying for unused services; periodic audits recover 10-15% of monthly spend (Arkes & Blumer, 1985).

tips.action: Set up one automatic savings transfer, even if it's just $10/month.
tips.science: Automation removes the need for willpower; pre-commitment devices increase savings rates by 81% (Thaler & Benartzi, 2004).

tips.action: Before your next purchase, wait 24 hours. If you still want it tomorrow, buy it.
tips.science: The 'cooling off' period reduces impulse purchases by 35% by allowing prefrontal cortex override of emotional spending (Hoch & Loewenstein, 1991).

tips.action: Spend 5 minutes reviewing your company's benefits page. You might be leaving money on the table.
tips.science: Benefit under-utilization averages 30-40% in large companies; awareness is the primary barrier (SHRM, 2022).

tips.action: Name one thing you did today that aligns with your personal values.
tips.science: Values-affirmation exercises buffer stress responses and improve performance under threat (Cohen & Sherman, 2014).

tips.action: Write down 3 things you're grateful for. Be specific.
tips.science: Gratitude journaling increases subjective well-being by 25% over 10 weeks (Emmons & McCullough, 2003).

tips.action: Ask yourself: In 5 years, will this task matter? Zoom out for 10 seconds.
tips.science: Temporal distancing reduces emotional intensity of current stressors (Bruehlman-Senecal & Ayduk, 2015).

tips.action: Do one small act of kindness today — hold a door, compliment a stranger, buy someone coffee.
tips.science: Prosocial spending and behavior increase happiness more than self-focused spending (Dunn et al., 2008).

tips.action: Spend 2 minutes in silence. No phone, no screen, no input. Just be.
tips.science: Two minutes of silence increases BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) and promotes hippocampal neurogenesis (Kirste et al., 2015).
